draft.introduction: The Descent of Ishtar to the Underworld is one of the oldest surviving narratives of divine self-emptying. The goddess Ishtar—Queen of Heaven, goddess of love and war—descends through the seven gates of the underworld to confront her sister Ereshkigal, Queen of the Dead. At each gate she is stripped of one article of her divine regalia: crown, earrings, necklace, breast ornaments, girdle, bracelets, and finally her garment. She arrives before Ereshkigal naked and powerless, and is killed and hung on a hook. The Akkadian text (a condensed version of the older Sumerian Descent of Inanna) survives on tablets from Nineveh and Assur. The theological structure is remarkable: divinity can only enter the realm of death by being stripped of divinity. The goddess must become not-goddess in order to reach the deepest place. This is the same logic that Paul articulates in Philippians 2:6–8—the divine figure who “emptied himself” (kenosis) and descended to the point of death. The parallel is not coincidental; it is structural.

translation.text: To the Land of No Return, the realm of Ereshkigal, Ishtar daughter of Sin set her mind. The daughter of Sin set her mind to the dark house, the dwelling of Irkalla, to the house which none who enter leave, to the road from which there is no way back.
translation.original_text: a-na KUR.NU.GI₄ qa-aq-qa-ri la ta-a-ri dŠTAR mārat d30 u-zu-un-ša iš-ku-un

translation.text: When she arrived at the gate of the Land of No Return, she said to the gatekeeper: “Open your gate for me, open your gate that I may enter! If you do not open the gate so that I may enter, I will smash the door, I will shatter the bolt, I will raise up the dead and they shall eat the living!”
translation.original_text: a-na bāb KUR.NU.GI₄ i-na ka-ša-di-ša a-na a-ti-i bā-bi a-mat iz-za-kar pi-ta-a bā-ba-ka lu-ru-ba a-na-ku

translation.text: At the first gate he removed the great crown from her head. “Why, gatekeeper, have you taken the great crown from my head?” “Enter, my lady. The rules of the Mistress of the Underworld are thus.” At the second gate he removed her earrings. At the third gate he removed her necklace. At the fourth gate he removed the ornaments from her breast. At the fifth gate he removed the girdle of birthstones from her waist. At the sixth gate he removed the bangles from her wrists and ankles. At the seventh gate he removed the garment of her body.
translation.original_text: i-na bābi iš-te-en šu-šu-ra-a agû-a rabî-ta ša qa-qa-di-ša u-še-li am-mi-ni at-mu-u bā-bu agû-a rabî-ta ša qa-qa-di-ia tu-še-li

translation.text: When Ishtar had descended to the Land of No Return, Ereshkigal saw her and was enraged at her presence. Ishtar, without thinking, rushed at her. Ereshkigal opened her mouth and spoke, to Namtar her vizier she gave the command: “Strike her! Loose against her the sixty miseries!”
translation.original_text: dŠTAR i-na e-re-bi-ša a-na KUR.NU.GI₄ dEreš-ki-gal i-mu-rši-ma i-na pa-ni-ša i-zi-iz

translator_notes.note: The seven gates correspond to the seven layers of divine identity being removed. Each article of clothing or jewelry is not merely decorative but a melammu—a component of the divine radiance that marks a god as a god. As each piece is removed, Ishtar becomes progressively less divine. The stripping is systematic: crown (sovereignty), earrings (divine hearing), necklace (divine speech), breast ornaments (nurturing power), girdle (fertility), bracelets (divine action), garment (the divine body itself). By the seventh gate she is naked—not just unclothed but de-divinised.

Ishtar’s threat to “raise up the dead and they shall eat the living” is not bluster. As goddess of both love and war, she holds power over the boundary between life and death. The threat reveals that the underworld’s gates exist not to keep the dead in but to keep the gods out. The entire architecture of death is a containment system for divinity. This inverts the usual assumption: it is not mortality that is the problem but immortality in the wrong place.
